agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order lifecycle management — specifically the cancellation policy governing when and whether a placed order can be cancelled before fulfillment. Three stakeholder groups (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided conflicting requirements about cancellation eligibility.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shipping, and delivery."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing its commitments. The precise conditions under which this is permitted are currently in dispute."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document representing a binding charge for an order. Finance asserts invoicing is irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Product asserts this is the cancellation cutoff."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of a customer submitting an order. Operations asserts invoicing occurs at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — under Product's rule this succeeds (not yet shipped); under Finance+Ops rules this fails (already invoiced at placement). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but shipment is delayed for days","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Product expects a wide cancellation window; Finance+Ops rules mean the window was already zero at placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"Cannot be defined until the primary contradiction is resolved."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we follow Product's rule and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance must support invoice reversal (credit notes, refund flows), which they explicitly say is not possible. If we follow Finance+Ops, cancellation is effectively impossible, which will increase chargebacks, customer complaints, and support costs.","operational":"HIGH — The resolution determines whether the finance team must build invoice reversal capabilities, or whether the operations team must decouple invoicing from placement, or whether the product team must abandon the cancellation feature entirely.","user":"HIGH — Customers expect to cancel orders that haven't shipped. If cancellation is impossible from the moment of placement, customer satisfaction and retention will suffer significantly.","risk":"CRITICAL — Implementing any one interpretation without stakeholder alignment will violate at least one team's stated constraint. Shipping the wrong behavior could create financial reconciliation errors, regulatory exposure, or a broken customer experience.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders if Finance+Ops interpretation wins. Will face confusing UX if rules are inconsistent."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must support credit notes / invoice reversal if Product interpretation wins, contradicting their stated irreversibility constraint."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to decouple invoicing from placement if a cancellation window is required, changing their pricing-lock strategy."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement may be impossible as specified without changes from Finance or Ops."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of whichever policy is chosen — either explaining why cancellation is impossible or processing complex reversal flows."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholder groups have provided requirements that form a logical contradiction. Taken together: (1) cancellable before shipment, (2) not cancellable once invoiced, and (3) invoiced at placement — these mean orders are both cancellable and not cancellable between placement and shipment. No amount of analysis can resolve this without a business decision from the stakeholders. Confidence is very low because no correct implementation can be defined from these specs.","high_confidence_areas":["The three statements are genuinely contradictory — this is not an interpretation issue","The contradiction is between Finance's irreversibility claim and Product's cancellation window, bridged by Ops' invoicing timing"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's constraint should yield","Whether 'invoicing' in Finance's language means the same thing as 'invoicing' in Operations' language","Whether there is an unstated business process (e.g., credit notes, provisional invoices) that could reconcile these rules"],"assumptions":["All three statements are intended as hard requirements by their respective teams","'Invoice' means the same thing across Finance and Operations contexts","There is no existing invoice reversal or credit note mechanism in play"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. Product says: cancellable before shipment. Finance says: not cancellable once invoiced. Operations says: invoiced at placement. Combining these: orders are invoiced the instant they are placed, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, therefore orders can NEVER be cancelled — directly contradicting Product's requirement that orders are cancellable before shipment. The cancellation window is simultaneously 'placement to shipment' and 'zero'.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Finance yields — introduce invoice reversal (credit notes) so that invoiced orders CAN be cancelled before shipment, preserving Product's requirement and Ops' invoicing timing.","B: Operations yields — decouple invoicing from placement (e.g., invoice at shipment or after a cancellation window closes), preserving Product's cancellation window and Finance's irreversibility rule.","C: Product yields — remove or severely restrict cancellation (e.g., only draft/unpaid orders), accepting that the invoicing-at-placement model makes post-placement cancellation impossible.","D: Redefine terms — explore whether Ops' 'invoicing' is actually a pricing lock or hold distinct from Finance's 'invoice', which may resolve the apparent contradiction through language clarification."],"recommendation":"Option D first (15-minute stakeholder alignment call to verify whether the same word means the same thing), then Option B as the most domain-correct fallback. In logistics, invoicing at placement is unusual — most systems invoice at or after shipment. A pricing lock can be achieved without a formal invoice, preserving both the cancellation window and price integrity."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria — the specification contains a logical contradiction that makes any implementation simultaneously correct and incorrect depending on which stakeholder's rule is honored.","business_rule_broken":"PM cancellation window vs. Finance invoicing irreversibility vs. Ops invoicing timing form an impossible triangle."}]}
